opensource.title: Open Source Countdown
opensource.subtitle: Kaitu is going open source
opensource.description: We are committed to open-sourcing Kaitu's core code, allowing everyone to view, verify, and contribute code to build a more secure and transparent network tool together.
opensource.releasedDesc: Kaitu's core code is now open source on GitHub. Visit our repository to view code, submit issues, or contribute code.
opensource.reason1Desc: Open source code allows everyone to review our implementation, ensuring there are no backdoors or malicious code, building genuine trust.
opensource.reason2Desc: Open source enables developers worldwide to participate, submit improvements and fixes, making the product better.
opensource.reason3Desc: More eyes mean faster discovery and fixing of security vulnerabilities. Open source software is often more secure than closed source.
opensource.reason4Desc: We believe technology should benefit everyone. Open source allows everyone to learn and use advanced network technologies.
opensource.whoDesc: Audit requests must be verified by Kaitu before they can proceed. The following three types of entities are authorized to initiate a formal audit:
opensource.who1Desc: A standing body composed of independent security experts that can proactively initiate audits by authority, without additional application. Audit findings are mandatorily published.
opensource.who2Desc: Operators and enterprise clients who have signed partnership agreements with Kaitu can submit audit requests through the partner channel. Findings are shared within the scope of the agreement.
opensource.who3Desc: Third-party organizations holding internationally recognized security certifications (such as ISO 27001, CREST, or OWASP-certified bodies) can initiate specialized audits after credential verification.
